evals.query: Our dashboard feels cluttered and overwhelming, but we can't remove any of the data — users need all of it. How do we make it feel calmer without cutting information?
expected_behavior: Distinguishes visual noise from information density — calm is achievable without removing data
Recommends using visual weight as a ranking system: work content dominates, chrome/navigation recedes
Suggests auditing borders, icons, backgrounds, and separators — each must earn its place by clarifying a relationship
Mentions 'structure should be felt, not seen' and that unnoticed refinements are a good sign
Advises testing against real, full, and empty states across light/dark/custom themes
Does NOT recommend simply deleting features or data to reduce clutter

evals.query: Several enterprise customers are asking us to add required fields and multiple assignees per task. Should we build these?
expected_behavior: Frames requests as input, not instructions — interpret the underlying need
Notes these are classic manager-driven requests that degrade the daily IC workflow
Explains Linear's stance of refusing whole categories of requests that protect managers at IC expense
Recommends finding the real problem (e.g., what the required field is meant to ensure) and solving that, or saying no
Invokes opinionated-software / 'one really good way' reasoning
Avoids defaulting to 'build it because customers asked'

evals.query: I asked an AI tool to generate a settings screen and it produced something that looks really polished. Can we just ship this?
expected_behavior: Invokes 'output isn't design' — polished form does not mean solved problem or good fit
Asks whether the underlying problem and forces (user needs, constraints, edge cases, workflows) were defined first
Recommends using AI for exploration/prototyping while keeping human judgment on problem framing and fit
Bonus (not required): references 'settings are not a design failure' — settings for genuine preferences, not to dodge product decisions
Advises validating against real app states rather than an isolated mockup
Does NOT treat visual polish as sufficient evidence of quality

evals.query: We want our app to feel instant and effortless. We're planning to add nicer loading skeletons and spinner animations. Good plan?
expected_behavior: Reframes: 'calm, dense, fast' is architecture and input design, not polish — skeletons/spinners add noise
Recommends eliminating the wait (optimistic local writes, local-first data) instead of decorating it
Raises the input-model dimension: fast backend still loses if the primary action needs a mouse and several menus
Suggests keyboard-first paths and a command palette over local data
Notes near-zero latency is what lets the UI stay minimal (no loading affordances needed)
Does NOT endorse better spinners as the path to feeling fast

evals.query: Our product has accumulated years of UI cruft and feels inconsistent. Leadership wants a big redesign. How should we approach scope and process?
expected_behavior: Names design debt and argues some debt is best paid in larger holistic sweeps, not per-screen fixes
Says the redesign should start from product evolution, not visual taste
Advises keeping the user's mental model stable and cutting high-risk scope (e.g., navigation changes) when risk is too high
Recommends design + engineering working together daily, no handoff
Suggests north-star/concept prototypes, stress-testing across environments and themes, and phased rollout (private beta then percentage rollout)
Mentions getting leadership backing for a holistic sweep rather than per-screen patching

evals.query: We're adding an AI assistant to our product. The plan is a chat sidebar where users type requests. Is chat the right interface?
expected_behavior: Challenges chat-by-default — generic chat is a weak, imprecise form for many workflows
Recommends a structured 'workbench' where AI operates inside clear context
Says to design review, approval, context, and control surfaces, not just the prompt box
For agentic flows, keeps a human accountable and surfaces reasoning/tool calls/elicitations
Uses the 'river without banks' idea — unbounded AI is powerful but directionless
Does NOT simply affirm the chat sidebar as the obvious choice

evals.query: How should we run design reviews and decide what ships? Right now we have weekly formal review meetings and we A/B test most changes before launch.
expected_behavior: Describes Linear's taste-driven model: no formal design reviews, async feedback in project channels
Explains that A/B tests are not used as decision-makers — taste decides, dogfooding and betas validate
Recommends feature flags to internal dogfooding within days, then optional customer beta
Mentions senior taste/sponsors applied continuously, not at checkpoints
Notes 'decide and move on' and that speed comes from competence, not corner-cutting
Connects this to no-handoff small teams that own problems end to end

evals.query: A stakeholder keeps giving contradictory feedback on our new feature and the project is dragging. What's going wrong and how do we unstick it?
expected_behavior: Identifies an unclear or contested problem definition as the likely root cause
Notes contradictory feedback often means people are reacting to different problem definitions
Recommends writing the problem in your own words and confirming it's the real problem, not a symptom
Separates problem design, conceptual solution, and execution — settle concepts before UI details
Asks diagnostic questions (is this real? what if we don't do it? who defined it?)
Does NOT jump straight to more mockups or visual iteration

evals.query: Two teams disagree on whether comments should show newest-first or oldest-first, so we're about to add a sort toggle in settings. Right call?
expected_behavior: Distinguishes a genuine recurring preference from punting a product decision into a toggle
Asks whether one ordering is actually better for the core reading/replying workflow, and recommends picking that default first
Notes settings aren't automatically a failure and can teach power users what's possible
Warns against settings becoming a dumping ground for unresolved decisions
Does NOT reflexively add the toggle, nor reflexively forbid all settings

evals.query: We're renaming 'tasks' to 'Sparks' and 'projects' to 'Constellations' to give the product a distinctive brand identity. Good idea?
expected_behavior: Invokes vocabulary as a design surface and the cost of invented jargon (users need a handbook)
Argues for universal, plain terms so nobody has to learn a private language
Asks what the rename clarifies for the user — distinctiveness alone isn't a reason
Notes invented terms mean different things to different people
Does NOT endorse renaming core objects for novelty or branding

evals.query: Our bug backlog has 1,400 open issues and keeps growing. We triage quarterly and only assign polish/UI bugs to the design team. How should we handle quality?
expected_behavior: Recommends a strict bug SLA and no permanent backlog (fix fast or it never gets fixed)
Frames quality as everyone's job, not just the design team's
Suggests a recurring quality ritual and group UI review (different people catch different defects)
Treats papercuts as real, scheduled work
Locates quality in hiring and no-handoff ownership, not in adding a separate QA gate
Does NOT endorse a permanent quarterly-triaged backlog as acceptable

evals.query: We're positioning the product as a flexible platform for any team, configurable to fit each company's process. Is that a strong strategy?
expected_behavior: Warns that generic 'for everyone / configurable to anything' positioning tends to be invisible
Recommends picking a specific user and use case and optimizing hard for it
Frames restraint and opinionation as the strategy, not a limitation
May note being most opinionated at the atomic level while taking more cues at broad container levels (optional)
Does NOT affirm maximal flexibility as the path to winning trust

evals.query: I have an approved Figma spec for our new settings page. Implement it in React with Tailwind, matching the spec exactly, and fix the flexbox overflow on the right panel.
expected_behavior: Does NOT drive the answer with Linear product-philosophy framing — the problem and design are already settled
Treats this as pixel/implementation execution that a frontend craft/implementation skill should own
At most offers a light pointer (e.g. test against real/empty states) without re-litigating the product decision
Does NOT re-open problem framing, opinionation, or 'is this the right problem' for an approved spec
